title: Beyond the SBOM: A Practical Supply-Chain Security Playbook for 2026
excerpt: Static SBOMs aren't enough anymore. Here's how defenders combine provenance, signing, VEX, and dependency hygiene to actually secure the modern build pipeline.
bodyHtml:
For years, the software bill of materials was treated as the finish line of supply-chain security. Generate an SBOM, attach it to a release, check the compliance box, move on. In 2026 that mindset is quietly failing. Attackers have shifted from chasing big-name vendors to poisoning the open-source plumbing every team depends on, and a list of ingredients does nothing to stop a malicious package that was never on the list in the first place. The industry has moved past the visibility era into something harder: continuous governance of everything that flows into your builds.

Why static SBOMs fall short

An SBOM is a snapshot. It tells you what was in an artifact at the moment it was generated, but it says nothing about whether those components were tampered with, whether a transitive dependency was swapped after the scan, or whether a known vulnerability is actually reachable in your code. Worse, SBOM quality is wildly inconsistent because so many upstream projects never publish their own, leaving downstream teams to reconstruct an incomplete picture.

Practitioners report two recurring frustrations: accuracy concerns, and the difficulty of wiring SBOM data into existing vulnerability management workflows. A document that nobody can act on is theater, not security.

  • No provenance: a list of names does not prove where a binary came from or who built it.
  • No exploitability context: raw CVE counts overwhelm teams without telling them what is actually dangerous.
  • Point-in-time only: dependencies and advisories change daily; a static file goes stale almost immediately.
Treat the SBOM as the beginning of your supply-chain program, not the end of it. The real work is proving integrity and acting on what you find.

Build verifiable provenance with SLSA and signing

The most important upgrade defenders can make is moving from "here is a list" to "here is proof." The SLSA framework gives you a maturity ladder for build integrity, and the central idea is provenance: a tamper-evident record of how an artifact was produced, signed by the build system rather than a developer's laptop. When provenance is generated by a hardened, ephemeral build environment, an attacker who compromises a single engineer cannot silently alter what ships to production.

Pair provenance with artifact signing. Sigstore has matured from an experiment into an operational default, letting teams sign containers, packages, and provenance with short-lived, identity-bound keys instead of long-lived secrets that leak. The defensive goal is simple to state and demanding to achieve: every binary that reaches production should carry a verifiable chain of custody, and your deployment gate should refuse anything that fails verification.

A pragmatic adoption order

  • Start by signing your own first-party artifacts and verifying signatures at deploy time.
  • Generate build provenance from your CI system, not from interactive sessions.
  • Enforce policy gradually: warn first, then block unsigned or unverifiable artifacts.
  • Record everything to an immutable log so incidents can be reconstructed.

Make vulnerability data actionable with VEX and reachability

A modern program enriches its inventory rather than just storing it. Vulnerability Exploitability eXchange (VEX) lets you assert, in machine-readable form, whether a given CVE actually affects your product. That single change collapses noise: instead of triaging thousands of theoretical findings, your team focuses on the handful that are genuinely exploitable in your context.

Reachability analysis pushes this further by correlating a flagged component with whether the vulnerable code path is ever invoked. The combination of continuously updated inventory, VEX statements, and reachability is what turns supply-chain data from a compliance artifact into a remediation engine. Defenders who adopt this stop drowning in alerts and start shipping fixes that matter, and just as importantly, they can explain to auditors and customers why a given advisory does not apply rather than scrambling to patch something that was never exploitable.

Harden the registries and the humans

Open-source ecosystems are now operating at a scale that defeats manual oversight. Package registries ingest enormous volumes of new releases every day, and automated consumption by AI coding tools accelerates both the production and the blind installation of dependencies. That scale is exactly what typosquatting, dependency-confusion, and account-takeover campaigns exploit. Your defenses have to assume the registry is hostile by default.

  • Pin and verify: lock dependencies to specific versions with integrity hashes, and review lockfile changes like code.
  • Use a curated proxy: route installs through an internal registry that quarantines new or suspicious packages before developers can pull them.
  • Scope publishing tokens: require phishing-resistant authentication for maintainers and use short-lived, least-privilege credentials for automated publishing.
  • Watch for behavioral red flags: install-time scripts, sudden maintainer changes, and network calls during build are classic supply-chain tells.

Don't forget the AI supply chain

The newest frontier is the model itself. Teams increasingly ship machine-learning systems, and those carry their own provenance questions: where did the training data come from, what weights were loaded, and were they tampered with in transit? The emerging practice is to extend bill-of-materials thinking to models, documenting architecture decisions, data sources, and safety benchmarks so that a poisoned model is as auditable as a poisoned package. The same provenance and signing disciplines apply: sign model weights, record where they came from, and verify them before they load into an inference service. Treating AI artifacts as first-class supply-chain citizens now will save painful retrofits later, because the registries that distribute models are already attracting the same kinds of malicious uploads that have plagued traditional package ecosystems.

The throughline across all of this is a shift in posture. Visibility was the question of the last decade; integrity and exploitability are the questions of this one. Defenders who sign their artifacts, prove their provenance, enrich findings with VEX and reachability, and treat their registries as untrusted territory will spend their energy on real risk instead of paperwork, and they will be far harder to compromise through the dependencies they never wrote.

title: Killing the Hardcoded Key: Secrets Management and Non-Human Identity in 2026
excerpt: Vaults alone won't save you. How defenders fight secrets sprawl with detection, short-lived credentials, and lifecycle governance for machine and AI identities.
bodyHtml:
The single most reliable way into a modern environment is not a zero-day; it is a credential someone left lying around. A leaked API token in a public commit, a database password baked into a container image, a long-lived service key copied into a configuration file that was never supposed to be shared. The uncomfortable truth of 2026 is that secrets sprawl has outpaced the vaults built to contain it, and the explosion of automated, non-human identities has turned a manageable problem into a sprawling governance challenge.

The new shape of the problem

For most of the last decade, the advice was simple: put your secrets in a vault. That advice is still correct and still insufficient. A centralized, encrypted vault should be your source of truth, with workloads authenticating against policy and retrieving secrets over TLS while every access is logged. But a vault only governs the secrets you actually put in it. It cannot see the credentials that have already escaped into source code, build logs, ticketing systems, chat history, or configuration files for new tooling.

That escape problem is large. Recent research has documented tens of thousands of unique secrets leaking through configuration files for emerging AI integrations alone, a category that barely existed a couple of years ago. Every new tool your developers adopt is another place a credential can leak.

A secret that has left the vault is not a secret anymore. Detection has to assume leakage is constant, not exceptional.

Detect before attackers do

Because leakage is continuous, detection has to be continuous too. The defensive baseline is to scan everywhere a secret might land and to do it as early as possible in the workflow.

  • Pre-commit hooks: block a hardcoded secret before it ever reaches the repository, where it would live forever in history.
  • Pipeline scanning: catch secrets that slip past local checks during CI, including in build logs and artifacts.
  • Broad surface coverage: extend scanning to wikis, ticket systems, and chat exports, not just code.

Detection only matters if it triggers action. The moment a secret is found in the wrong place, treat it as compromised, full stop. Revoke it, rotate it, and investigate the blast radius: what could that credential reach, and was it used by anyone you cannot account for? A found secret is an incident, not a warning. The instinct to simply delete the offending commit is a trap, because git history preserves it and any clone already has a copy; the only safe assumption is that the secret is already in an attacker's hands and must be rendered useless.

Rotate without breaking production

Many teams avoid rotation because they fear the outage that follows. The fix is a rotation pattern designed for zero downtime rather than a heroic late-night swap. The dual-credential approach keeps both the old and the new credential valid during a brief overlap window. Services continue using the current secret until they naturally pick up the new one, and only once propagation is confirmed does the old credential get invalidated. Done well, rotation becomes a routine background event instead of a feared maintenance window.

Automate the schedule so rotation is not dependent on someone remembering. The goal is to make a stolen credential worthless within a short window simply because it expires, which dramatically shrinks the value of any single leak.

The non-human identity reckoning

The biggest shift in this space is who, or what, holds the credentials. CI/CD bots, infrastructure-as-code runners, microservice accounts, and now autonomous AI agents are created faster than any manual process can track. These non-human identities frequently outnumber human users by an order of magnitude, and they tend to accumulate broad, long-lived permissions that nobody ever revisits. Each one is a standing target.

Agentic AI makes this acute. An AI agent that can call tools, read data stores, and trigger actions is, from a security standpoint, a privileged service account that improvises. If it holds a static, over-scoped credential, a prompt-injection or logic flaw can turn it into an insider threat. The answer is to govern these identities with the same rigor you apply to humans, ideally more, because unlike a human an agent can act thousands of times a second and will not pause to question an instruction that looks unusual. Every credential you hand an agent should be assumed to be one manipulated prompt away from being used against you.

Build a non-human identity program

A durable program follows four repeatable steps, and the order matters because you cannot protect what you have not inventoried.

  • Discover: build a continuously updated inventory of every machine and agent identity across your environment, including the forgotten ones.
  • Understand: map what each identity can access, who owns it, and what it is actually supposed to do.
  • Enforce: apply least privilege, eliminate long-lived static credentials in favor of short-lived identity-driven access, and remediate excessive permissions.
  • Monitor: watch for anomalous behavior and respond to identity-based threats in real time.

The strategic direction is clear: move away from long-lived static secrets entirely. Where a workload can authenticate using a short-lived, identity-bound token issued just in time, there is no standing secret to steal. Vaulting becomes the default developer experience, every service account and agent becomes a governed identity with a lifecycle, and the attack surface shrinks because there is simply less to leak. Vaults remain the foundation, but in 2026 the winning programs treat detection, ruthless rotation, and non-human identity governance as the rest of the building.

title: Securing the Agent: A Defender's Guide to LLM and Agentic AI Application Security
excerpt: Prompt injection is the new injection flaw. Defend LLM and agentic apps with least-privilege tooling, output filtering, and human-in-the-loop controls.
bodyHtml:
Every team shipping a feature powered by a large language model has quietly added a new and unusual component to its architecture: a system that follows instructions written in plain language, from sources it cannot fully control. That flexibility is the entire value proposition, and it is also the core security problem. When an application both reads untrusted data and acts on instructions found in that data, the boundary between content and command erodes. Prompt injection is the defining application-security risk of the AI era, and the rise of autonomous agents has raised the stakes considerably.

Why prompt injection is so hard to fix

Traditional injection flaws have a clean fix: separate code from data with parameterized queries or strict encoding. Prompt injection resists that approach because, for a language model, instructions and data are the same thing. There is no reliable syntactic boundary to enforce. Security frameworks for LLM applications rank prompt injection as the top risk precisely because it can chain into far worse outcomes when the model is wired to tools that execute real actions.

There are two flavors defenders need to understand. Direct injection is when a user crafts input that overrides intended behavior. Indirect injection is sneakier: the model ingests content from an external source, a web page, a document, an email, that carries hidden instructions, and those instructions hijack the model's behavior without the user ever seeing them.

You cannot fully prevent a model from being influenced by malicious text. You can, and must, limit what happens when it is.

Least privilege is your strongest control

If you take one principle from this article, take this: assume the model can be manipulated, and constrain what a manipulated model is able to do. Because an LLM can be steered by hostile input, the most effective defense is to make a successful manipulation boring, an agent that has no dangerous capabilities cannot cause dangerous outcomes.

  • Scoped credentials: give the agent the narrowest possible access tokens, never a broad admin key.
  • Allowlisted tools: expose only the specific functions the task requires, not a general-purpose execution surface.
  • Restricted data access: limit which records and systems the model can read, scoped per user and per session.
  • Rate limits and sandboxing: cap how often and how broadly an agent can act, and isolate tool execution.

This is the same least-privilege discipline that has protected service accounts for decades, applied to a new kind of actor. An agent should be treated as an untrusted, privileged process that happens to be persuadable.

Filter the inputs and the outputs

Constraining capabilities is necessary but not sufficient; you also want to reduce the odds of a successful injection and to catch bad outcomes before they propagate. Defense in depth here means treating both ends of the model as boundaries.

On the input side, sanitize and contextually isolate untrusted content. When the model must process external documents or user data, label that content clearly as untrusted and keep it separated from trusted system instructions. On the output side, never blindly trust what the model returns. If the output will be rendered in a browser, used in a database query, or passed to another system, validate and encode it exactly as you would any other untrusted input, because that is precisely what it is. A model that has been injected will happily produce a cross-site scripting payload or a malicious command if your downstream code executes its output verbatim.

Practical guardrails

  • Keep system prompts and tool definitions out of reach of user-controlled text.
  • Apply output encoding based on the destination, not the source.
  • Treat retrieved documents and tool results as tainted until validated.

Put a human in the loop for high-risk actions

Automation is the point of an agent, but full automation of consequential actions is where breaches happen. The defensive pattern is to require explicit human approval before an agent does anything irreversible or sensitive: moving money, deleting data, sending external communications, or changing access controls. A confirmation step converts a silent compromise into a visible request that a person can reject. The friction is the feature.

Pair approvals with thorough audit logging. Every tool call, every input the agent acted on, and every decision should be recorded so that when something does go wrong, you can reconstruct exactly what the agent saw and did. Without that trail, investigating an agent incident is guesswork.

Test adversarially and watch the agentic frontier

Security for these systems is not a one-time review; it is an ongoing practice of trying to break your own application before someone else does. Adversarial testing, sometimes called AI red teaming, probes the model with crafted inputs, jailbreak attempts, and indirect-injection scenarios to find where your guardrails fail. Because models and prompts change frequently, this testing needs to be continuous and ideally automated as part of your release process.

The agentic shift adds new dimensions. Autonomy, tool integration, and multi-agent coordination amplify the blast radius of any single failure: an agent that can spawn or instruct other agents can turn one compromised step into a cascade. Defenders should scrutinize the permissions of every agent in a chain, limit how agents can influence one another, and assume that any data crossing between them could carry an injected instruction.

None of this requires abandoning AI features. It requires treating an LLM-powered application as what it is, a system that takes instructions from untrusted sources, and applying the timeless defensive disciplines accordingly: minimize privilege, distrust input and output, gate dangerous actions behind human judgment, log everything, and keep testing. The teams that internalize this now will ship AI features that hold up, while those that bolt a powerful model onto broad permissions will learn the hard way.

title: From Posture to Risk: Modernizing Cloud Security with CSPM and CNAPP in 2026
excerpt: Misconfigurations still cause most cloud breaches. Move from raw CSPM alerts to risk-based, owned, and verifiable remediation that actually closes the gaps.
bodyHtml:
The most common way organizations get breached in the cloud is embarrassingly mundane: a storage bucket left open to the internet, a security group that allows the world to reach a management port, an over-privileged role that an attacker pivots through. These are not sophisticated exploits. They are misconfigurations, and they persist because cloud environments change constantly and faster than any human can manually review. Cloud Security Posture Management exists to close that gap, but in 2026 the conversation has moved from simply finding misconfigurations to actually fixing the ones that matter.

What CSPM is actually for

Cloud Security Posture Management is the practice of continuously assessing cloud environments for misconfigurations, policy violations, exposed assets, weak identity permissions, and compliance gaps. Instead of a quarterly audit that is stale the moment it finishes, CSPM watches your infrastructure-as-a-service, platform, and SaaS footprint around the clock and flags drift from a secure baseline. The value is in the word continuous: in a multi-cloud environment where engineers deploy dozens of times a day, point-in-time checks simply cannot keep up.

The categories of problem it detects will be familiar to anyone who has run a cloud account at scale.

  • Public exposure: storage buckets or blob containers open to anonymous access.
  • Overly permissive networking: security groups allowing unrestricted ingress on sensitive ports.
  • Excessive permissions: roles and service accounts with wildcard policies far beyond what they need.
  • Missing protections: unencrypted volumes and databases, or resources with no logging enabled.
Finding a misconfiguration is the easy part. The hard part is making sure the right person fixes it and that it stays fixed.

The alert-fatigue trap

Here is where many programs stall. A CSPM tool turned loose on a large estate will generate thousands of findings, and a flat list of thousands of "high" severities is functionally useless. Teams burn out triaging noise, the most dangerous issues hide among the trivial ones, and nothing gets meaningfully safer. The problem is not detection; it is prioritization and ownership.

This is what drove the rise of risk-based analysis and the consolidation of tooling into broader platforms. A risk-based view does not just tell you a bucket is public; it correlates signals to answer the question that actually matters: which issue is most likely to lead to a real breach? A public bucket that contains classified personal data, reachable by an identity that can escalate privileges, is a five-alarm fire. A public bucket holding marketing images is not. Treating them identically guarantees you will mis-spend your limited remediation hours.

CSPM, CNAPP, and the move to context

CSPM focuses on posture: configuration, exposure, compliance, and remediation. The broader Cloud-Native Application Protection Platform wraps that posture management together with workload protection, identity and entitlement management, infrastructure-as-code scanning, container and Kubernetes security, and runtime detection. The industry is converging hard on this consolidation, with analysts projecting that the large majority of enterprises will run their cloud security through a single integrated platform, up from a small minority a few years ago.

The reason is context. Posture data on its own is flat. When you correlate a misconfiguration with the sensitivity of the data behind it, the permissions of the identities that can reach it, and whether the workload is actually running and exposed, you get a prioritized list of genuine risk instead of an undifferentiated pile of alerts. That correlation is what separates a program that reduces breach likelihood from one that just produces dashboards.

Shift left into infrastructure-as-code

The cheapest misconfiguration to fix is the one that never deploys. Because most cloud resources are now defined in code through templates and modules, the same defensive logic that applies to application security applies here: catch the problem in the pull request, not in production. Scanning infrastructure-as-code before it is applied lets you block an open security group or an unencrypted database while it is still a few lines in a file, where the fix is a one-line change and no data has been exposed.

Pairing pre-deployment IaC scanning with runtime CSPM gives you a guardrail on both ends: prevent known-bad configurations from shipping, and catch the drift, manual changes, and emergent exposures that inevitably occur in live environments. Neither alone is enough; together they cover the full lifecycle of a cloud resource.

Make remediation real with ownership

The detail that decides whether a cloud security program succeeds or fails is unglamorous: ownership. Enterprise CSPM falls apart when findings have no owner. A useful program does not just say "this resource is misconfigured"; it shows who owns the asset, which application depends on it, where the remediation ticket should go, and what evidence proves the issue was actually closed. Without that chain, findings rot in a backlog and the same problems resurface indefinitely.

  • Attribute every asset to a team and an application so findings route automatically.
  • Integrate with your workflow so a finding becomes a ticket in the system engineers already use.
  • Track evidence of closure so you can prove an issue was fixed, not just acknowledged.
  • Handle exceptions deliberately with documented, time-bound risk acceptances rather than silent ignores.

Cloud security in 2026 is less about buying a scanner and more about building an operating model. The tools to find misconfigurations are mature and widely available; the differentiator is whether your program can take a flood of raw findings, rank them by real risk, route them to the people who can act, prevent the worst ones from ever shipping, and prove that the dangerous gaps were genuinely closed. Do that, and the mundane misconfiguration stops being your most likely path to a breach.

title: Phishing-Resistant by Default: Building an Identity Security Program for the AI Phishing Era
excerpt: AI-powered phishing defeats legacy MFA in real time. Here's how defenders deploy passkeys and FIDO2 and close the fallback gaps that quietly undo them.
bodyHtml:
Multi-factor authentication was supposed to be the great equalizer, the control that made stolen passwords harmless. For a while it was. But the attackers adapted, and in 2026 the most common forms of MFA are no longer the reliable barrier they once were. Adversary-in-the-middle phishing kits now relay logins in real time and walk away with authenticated sessions, while AI-generated lures have pushed phishing success rates to levels that would have seemed alarmist a few years ago. The codes-and-prompts era of authentication is closing. What replaces it is phishing-resistant by design, and getting there is as much an organizational project as a technical one.

Why most MFA no longer holds

The weakness is structural, not a matter of user carelessness. SMS codes, email codes, and even push notifications all share a fatal property: the secret or approval can be relayed. An attacker stands up a convincing fake login page, the victim enters their password and their one-time code, and the attacker's automated kit immediately replays both to the real service, capturing the resulting session. The user did everything they were told to do, and it did not matter.

AI has industrialized this. Generated lures are fluent and personalized, fake pages are spun up at scale, and the relay happens in milliseconds. Push-based MFA adds its own failure mode, fatigue, where users approve a prompt simply to make the buzzing stop. Against this threat model, any factor a human can read and retype, or approve with a tap, is phishable.

If a credential can be relayed to a fake site and replayed to the real one, it is not phishing-resistant, no matter how many factors it involves.

What makes an authenticator phishing-resistant

The property that defeats relay attacks is origin binding. Phishing-resistant methods, principally FIDO2 security keys and passkeys, use public-key cryptography where the credential is cryptographically tied to the legitimate site's origin. When a user lands on a look-alike domain, the authenticator simply refuses to produce a usable response, because the origin does not match. There is nothing for the phishing kit to capture and nothing to replay. Security authorities have, for this reason, called FIDO2 and PKI-based methods the gold standard.

Passkeys are the consumer-friendly expression of the same standard. They replace passwords entirely with origin-bound key pairs that cannot be phished, shared, or replayed. They come in a few forms, and the distinction matters for risk decisions.

  • Hardware security keys: the strongest assurance, ideal for administrators and other privileged accounts.
  • Device-bound passkeys: stored in a device's secure hardware and non-exportable, well suited to corporate-managed devices.
  • Synced passkeys: backed up across a user's devices for convenience, a strong fit for the general workforce.

The fallback trap that undoes everything

Here is the failure that catches even sophisticated organizations: deploying phishing-resistant authentication while leaving weaker methods enabled as a fallback. If a user can register a passkey but still log in with an SMS code when the passkey is inconvenient, an attacker will simply steer the victim down the weaker path. The phishing-resistant method becomes optional, and optional security is the security you do not have.

The pattern for success is blunt: deploy phishing-resistant authentication and then remove the fallbacks. This is harder than it sounds because the weak paths hide in unexpected corners, account recovery flows, help-desk reset procedures, legacy applications that only speak older protocols, and break-glass admin accounts. Each of those is a place where an attacker can sidestep your strong front door, and each needs a deliberate plan rather than a quiet exception.

Where fallbacks usually hide

  • Self-service recovery that reverts to email or SMS verification.
  • Help-desk processes that reset MFA after a phone call.
  • Legacy authentication protocols that bypass modern policy.
  • Service and admin accounts exempted "temporarily" and then forgotten.

Roll it out by risk, not all at once

A practical deployment aligns the strength of the authenticator with the sensitivity of what it protects. Hardware keys for privileged access, device-bound passkeys for managed corporate devices, and synced passkeys for the broad workforce gives you strong coverage without demanding the same friction everywhere. Adaptive policies can reduce friction further by stepping up verification only when risk signals warrant it, but the floor must stay phishing-resistant, adaptive should never mean falling back to a phishable factor.

Recovery deserves the same rigor as primary login. Many breaches happen not at the front door but through a recovery workflow that quietly drops the security bar. Design recovery to enroll a new strong authenticator through a verified, attended process rather than reverting to a code sent over a phishable channel.

Identity as the foundation of zero trust

None of this exists in isolation. Phishing-resistant authentication is the cornerstone of a zero-trust posture, where every access request is verified continuously regardless of where it originates and no session is trusted simply because it succeeded once. When identity is the perimeter, the strength of your authentication directly bounds the strength of everything built on top of it. A weak authenticator undermines every downstream control; a phishing-resistant one gives them a solid foundation.

The roadmap for defenders is clear even if the execution is demanding. Treat phishing-resistant authentication as the target state, not a premium add-on. Roll it out across the workforce by risk tier. Then do the unglamorous, essential work of hunting down and closing every fallback, recovery shortcut, and legacy bypass that lets an attacker route around your strong authentication. In an era where AI has made convincing phishing nearly free to produce, the organizations that thrive will be the ones whose credentials simply cannot be phished in the first place, and who refused to leave a weaker door open beside the strong one.
